entries.etymology: The positive form is from Proto-Germanic *gamalaz. The comparative and superlative forms are from Proto-Germanic *alþizô and *alþistaz respectively, comparison forms of the positive *aldaz (“old”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₂eltós, ultimately from the root *h₂el- (“to grow, nourish”). Compare with English old – elder – eldest.
glosses: old, aged; of a certain age

From Proto-Germanic *gudą (“god”). Compare Old Saxon, Old Frisian, and Old English god, Old High German and Old Dutch got, Gothic 𐌲𐌿𐌸 (guþ).

From Proto-Germanic *habjaną (“to have, hold”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *kap- (“to take, seize”). Cognate with Old English habban, Old Frisian hebba, Old Saxon hebbian, Old High German habēn, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌱𐌰𐌽 (haban).
From Proto-Germanic *habą.
From Proto-Germanic *haldaną.
From earlier haustr (masculine, like vetr (“winter”) and sumarr (“summer”)), from Proto-Germanic *harbustaz, variant of *harbistaz, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *kerp-.
Equivalent to hár (“high”) + degi (“day”).
From Proto-Germanic *hauhaz (“high”). Cognate with Old English hēah, Old Frisian hāch, Old Saxon hōh, Old High German hōh, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌿𐌷𐍃 (hauhs).
From Proto-Germanic *hērą (“hair”). Compare Old Saxon and Old High German hār, Old English her, hǣr.
From hár + tíð.

entries.etymology: Derived from the adjective heilbrigðr.
From Proto-Germanic *hailisōną (“to greet, salute”). Cognate with Old High German heilida.
From Proto-Germanic *hailisōną (“to greet, salute”). Cognate with Old High German heilida.
Inherited from Proto-Germanic *hailaz (“whole, safe, sound”), from Proto-Indo-European *kéh₂ilos (“healthy, whole”).
Borrowed from Middle Low German herberge, from Old Saxon heriberga, from Proto-West Germanic *harjabergu.

From Old Saxon hērro, from Old Dutch hēriro, Old High German hērro, the comparative form of hēr (“noble, venerable”) (German hehr), by analogy with Latin senior (“elder”). The Old High German word originally meant "grey, grey-haired", and descends from Proto-Germanic *hairaz (“grey”), making it cognate with Old English hār (English hoar), Old Norse hárr.

From Old Saxon hērro, from Old Dutch hēriro, Old High German hērro, the comparative form of hēr (“noble, venerable”) (German hehr), by analogy with Latin senior (“elder”). The Old High German word originally meant "grey, grey-haired", and descends from Proto-Germanic *hairaz (“grey”), making it cognate with Old English hār (English hoar), Old Norse hárr.
glosses: to confer with the title of "herra" upon a person

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *hauzijaną, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂ḱh₂owsyéti. Cognate with Old English hīeran, Old Frisian hera, Old Saxon hōrian, Old High German hōren, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌿𐍃𐌾𐌰𐌽 (hausjan).
From Proto-Germanic *hē₂r.

From a merger of several words: Proto-Norse *ᚺᛁᚾᚨᛉ (*hinaʀ), cognate with Ancient Greek ἐκεῖνος (ekeînos), and Proto-Norse *ᛖᚾᚨᛉ (*enaʀ), irregularly from Proto-Germanic *jainaz (“that over there, yon”), whence also Old English ġeon, Old Frisian jen, jena, Old High German jēner, Gothic 𐌾𐌰𐌹𐌽𐍃 (jains).
From Proto-Norse ᚺᛁᚾᛟ (hino), from the accusative form of Proto-Germanic *hiz, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱís, compound of *ḱe + *ís.
From or related to Proto-Germanic *haitį̄. See also heitr (“hot”).
From Proto-Germanic *hittijaną (“to come upon, find”), from Proto-Indo-European *kh₂eyd- (“to cut, strike, fall, fall upon”). The participle forms are of the same verb.
Originally the dative of *hjá (“household”) (from Proto-Germanic *hīwô), thus literally "at home"; compare hjú, hjó, hjón.

From Proto-Germanic *hlaupaną, whence also Old English hlēapan ( > English leap), Old Frisian hlapa ( > West Frisian ljeppe), Old Saxon hlōpan ( > Low German lopen), Dutch lopen, Old High German hlaufan ( > German laufen). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *klewb- (“to spring, stumble”).
Inherited from Proto-Norse ᚺᛚᚨᛁᚹᚨ n (hlaiwa, “grave”), from Proto-Germanic *hlaiwą. Possibly more closely related to Gothic 𐌷𐌻𐌹𐌾𐌰 (hlija, “booth, tent”).
From Proto-Germanic *hleuzą (“cheek, ear”), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱlews- (“temple of the forehead, cheek”). More at leer.
glosses: the sides (cheeks) of an axe blade, knife, or bodice

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *hliwjaz, *hlēwaz, *hlēwijaz (“warm”), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱal(w)e-, *ḱel(w)e-, *k(')lēw- (“warm, hot”). Cognate with Old English hlēow (“warm, sunny”). More at lukewarm.
From Proto-Germanic *hnuts, from Proto-Indo-European *knew-. Cognate with Old English hnutu (English nut), Dutch noot, Old High German nuz (German Nuss).
From Proto-Germanic *hrawaz.

Old Norse hreinn
Inherited from Proto-Germanic *hrainaz, most likely derived from Proto-Indo-European *ḱérh₂-s (“horn”). Akin to German Ren (“reindeer”) and Old English hrān (“reindeer”).
From Proto-Germanic *hrainiz.
From Proto-Germanic *hrainisōną (“clean, cleanse, purify”).
From Proto-Germanic *hringijaną.
From Proto-Germanic *hringijǭ. Related to hringr (“ring, circle”).
From Proto-Germanic *hrōpaną.

Verbal derivate of Old Norse hugr (“mind, thought”). Compare Swedish komma ihåg (“remember”, literally “come to mind”), ihågkomma (“remember”), hågkomst (“memory, recollection”); English come to mind.
From Proto-Germanic *hurdiz, from Proto-Indo-European *kreh₂-. Cognate to English hurdle.
From Proto-Germanic *hūsą, whence also Old English hūs (English house), Old Frisian hūs, Old Dutch hūs (Dutch huis), Old High German hūs (German Haus, Old Saxon hūs (German Low German Huus).
From hús + bóndi.
From Proto-Germanic *hwassaz.
From Proto-Germanic *hwargin, equivalent to hvar (“where”) + -gi (“not, naught”). Cognate with Old English hwerġen, Old Saxon hwergin.

entries.etymology: From hverr + -gi.
From Proto-Germanic *hwīlō (“period of rest”).
Form Proto-Germanic *hwīlāną (“to rest”).
glosses: to lie, rest, sleep (in a bed)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *in (“in, into”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁én (“in”).
From Proto-Germanic *kannǭ.

Uncertain; either:
* from Proto-Germanic *kaupōną (“to buy”), from *kaupô (“inkkeeper; merchant”), from Latin caupō (“tradesman”), thus a cognate of Gothic 𐌺𐌰𐌿𐍀𐍉𐌽 (kaupōn, “barter”); or
* of native origin, related to Gothic 𐌺𐌰𐌿𐍀𐌰𐍄𐌾𐌰𐌽 (kaupatjan, “strike; cuff”), which is morphologically compatible with kaupa.
Senses in other languages derived by relating striking (of hands) to trans...
From Proto-Germanic *kannijaną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵn̥néh₃ti, from *ǵneh₃- (“to know”).
Borrowed from Old English cirice, from Proto-West Germanic *kirikā, from Byzantine Greek κυριακόν (kuriakón, “Lord's house”), from Ancient Greek κύριος (kúrios, “lord”).
From Proto-Germanic *kwenǭ, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʷḗn (“woman”).
From Proto-Germanic *kustōną (“to try, taste”), from *kustuz, whence also Old Norse kostr.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *kussijaną, whence also Old English cyssan (English kiss), Old Frisian kessa, Old Saxon kussian, Dutch kussen, Old High German kussen (German küssen).

From Proto-Germanic *lagjaną (“to lay, place”). Cognate with Old English lecgan, Old Frisian ledza, lega, leia, Old Saxon leggian, Old Dutch leggen, Old High German leggen, Gothic 𐌻𐌰𐌲𐌾𐌰𐌽 (lagjan).
From Proto-Germanic *mainą.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *lesaną. The sense “read” is derived from West Germanic (Old Saxon or Old High German), a semantic loan of Latin legō (“gather, read”); for reading the Germanic runes, which were in use before the adoption of the Latin alphabet, the usual verb had been ráða (same as English read).

From Proto-Germanic *laubijaną, whence also Old English līfan, lēfan, lȳfan, Old High German gilouben (“believe”) and irlouben (“allow”) (German glauben, erlauben), Gothic 𐌿𐍃𐌻𐌰𐌿𐌱𐌾𐌰𐌽 (uslaubjan, “allow”). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *lewbʰ- (“to hold dear; love”).
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
From Proto-Germanic *laubiją. Related to leyfa, from Proto-Germanic *laubijaną. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *lewbʰ- (“to hold dear, love”).

From Proto-Germanic *libjaną (“to live, to be alive”), from Proto-Indo-European *leyp- (“to leave, cling, linger”). Cognate with Old English libban, Old Frisian leva, Old Saxon libbian, Old Dutch libben, Old High German lebēn, Gothic 𐌻𐌹𐌱𐌰𐌽 (liban).
From Proto-Germanic *ligjaną, from Proto-Indo-European *legʰ-. Compare Old English liċġan, Old Saxon liggian, Old Frisian lidzia, Old High German liggen, Gothic 𐌻𐌹𐌲𐌰𐌽 (ligan).

From Proto-Germanic *lītilaz (“small, little, few”). Cognate with Gothic 𐌻𐌴𐌹𐍄𐌹𐌻𐍃 (leitils). The comparative and superlative degrees are from Proto-Germanic *minnizô and *minnistaz respectively, ultimately being degrees of Proto-Indo-European *minu- (“small”), but are already inflections of *lītilaz at the Proto-Germanic stage.
From Proto-Germanic *lībą.
From Proto-Germanic *leuhsą. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *lewk- (“bright, shine”).

From Proto-Germanic *leuganą. Cognates with Old English lēoġan (Modern English lie), Old Saxon liogan, Dutch liegen, Old High German liogan (German lügen), Gothic 𐌻𐌹𐌿𐌲𐌰𐌽 (liugan). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *lewgʰ-. Indo-European cognates include Lithuanian lūgoti.
From Proto-Germanic *lukilaz. Akin to lúka + -ill.
Altered from earlier *lækir under the influence of the verb lækna, from Proto-Germanic *lēkijaz.
See the singular noun megin (“strength, power, might”)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *midi.
From Proto-Germanic *midjǭ (“centre”), *midją, whence also German Mitte.
From Proto-Germanic *mōdēr, from Proto-Indo-European *méh₂tēr. Compare Old Saxon mōdar, Old Frisian mōder, Old English mōdor, Old Dutch muoder, Old High German muoter.
From Proto-Germanic *mundiz (“memory, mind”).

From Proto-Germanic *namô (“name”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁nómn̥ (“name”). Cognate with Old English nama, noma, Old Frisian noma, Old Saxon namo, Old Dutch namo, Old High German namo, Gothic 𐌽𐌰𐌼𐍉 (namō).
Unclear, formed as if a past participle. Compare older nǫkkviðr, which this term largely displaced.
From Proto-Germanic *nēþlō.

Classic Norse form (11th c.), from earlier nȳʀ (9th c.), *ᚾᛁᚢᛦ (*niuʀ) (8th c.), from Proto-Norse *ᚾᛁᚹᛦ (*niwʀ) (7th c. syncope), ᚾᛁᚹᚨᛉ (niwaʀ) (5th c.), from Proto-Germanic *niwjaz, whence also and Old High German and Old Saxon niuwi, Old Frisian nīe, Old English nīewe (English new), Gothic 𐌽𐌹𐌿𐌾𐌹𐍃 (niujis). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *néwos.
From Proto-Germanic *upanōną.
From Proto-Germanic *upanōną.
From Proto-Germanic *wurdą, from Proto-Indo-European *wr̥dʰom, zero grade of Proto-Indo-European *werdʰo- (“word”), akin to Old English word, Old High German wort.
From Proto-Norse ᛟᛏᚨ (ota), from Proto-Germanic *uhtô, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂egʰ- (“to be upset, afraid”).

From Proto-Norse *ᚱᚨᛞᚨ n (*rada /⁠rāda⁠/), from Proto-Germanic *rēdą n, alternative form of *rēdaz m, whence Old English rǣd m, Old High German rāt m (“advice”) (German Rat m (“advice”)), Proto-Norse *ᚱᚨᛞᚨᛉ (*radaʀ /⁠*rādaʀ⁠/) (attested in personal name), Old Norse -ráðr.
From Proto-Norse *ᚱᚨᚢᚾᛁᛃᚨᚾ (*raunijan), from Proto-Germanic *raunijaną. Compare reynir (“tester”) (inherited from Proto-Norse ᚱᚨᚢᚾᛁᛃᚨᛉ (raunijaʀ, “Tester”, name of a spear)).

From Proto-Norse *ᚱᛁᛞᚨᚾ (*ridan), from Proto-Germanic *rīdaną, from Proto-Indo-European *reydʰ-. Cognate with Old English rīdan (English ride), Old Saxon rīdan (Low German rieden), Old Dutch rīdan (Dutch rijden), Old High German rītan (German reiten).

From Proto-Germanic *rūmą, from Proto-Indo-European *rewh₁-. Cognate with Old English rūm (English room), Old Saxon rūm (Low German Ruum), Dutch ruim, Old High German rūm (German Raum), Gothic 𐍂𐌿𐌼𐍃 (rūms).
From Proto-Germanic *samanai.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *sagjaną (“to say”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sekʷ- (“to say”). Cognate with Old English secġan, Old Frisian sedza, sidza, sega, Old Saxon seggian, Old High German sagēn.
From Proto-Germanic *saljaną.
From Proto-Germanic *salhijǭ, *salhô, whence also English sallow, and by borrowing, Finnish salava.

From Proto-Germanic *sandijaną, causative form of Proto-Indo-European *sent- (“to head for, go”) (so literally "to make someone go"). Compare Old Saxon sendian, Old Frisian senda, Old English sendan, Gothic 𐍃𐌰𐌽𐌳𐌾𐌰𐌽 (sandjan).
Causative of sitja. From Proto-Norse ᛊᚨᛏᛁᛞᛟ (satido) (1st singular past indicative), Proto-Germanic *satjaną, from Proto-Indo-European *sodéyeti.
From Proto-Germanic *sitjaną, akin to Old English sittan, Old Saxon sittian, Old Frisian sitta, Old High German sizzen, Gothic 𐍃𐌹𐍄𐌰𐌽 (sitan). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sed- (“sit”).
Contracted from Proto-Germanic *sīþuz (“late”) *þanō (instrumental form of *sa (“that”)), similar to Old English siþþan (“since”). See also Gothic 𐍃𐌴𐌹𐌸𐌿𐍃 (seiþus).
Contracted from Proto-Germanic *sīþuz (“late”) *þanō (instrumental form of *sa (“that”)), similar to Old English siþþan (“since”). See also Gothic 𐍃𐌴𐌹𐌸𐌿𐍃 (seiþus).
Contracted from Proto-Germanic *sīþuz (“late”) *þanō (instrumental form of *sa (“that”)), similar to Old English siþþan (“since”). See also Gothic 𐍃𐌴𐌹𐌸𐌿𐍃 (seiþus).

From earlier sási (attested in runic insriptions), originally the normal declension of sá + -si. Cognate with Old English þes (English this), Old High German dese (German diese).
glosses: this, that (referring to both persons and things)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *sehwaną (“to see”) (for cognates see there). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sekʷ- (“to see, notice”).
See sær.
Possibly from skil n (“difference”), related to the verb skilja (“to separate, discern”).

entries.etymology: Possibly from skil n (“difference”), related to the verb skilja (“to separate, discern”).

From Proto-Germanic *skiljaną (“to separate, limit”), from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kelH- (“to split, cut”). Compare Old Armenian ցելում (cʻelum). Cognate with Old English scylian, scielian (“to divide, separate”). More at skill.

From Proto-Germanic *skeutaną, from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kewd-. Compare Old English scēotan (English shoot), Old Frisian skiata (West Frisian sjitte), Old Saxon skiotan (Low German scheten), Old High German sciozzan (German schießen).
From Proto-Germanic *skuþōną (“to view”), a formation related to *skawwōną (“to look”) and *skauniz (“shining”), all from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kewh₁- (“to perceive”).

From Proto-Germanic *skrahō (“dry animal skin”), of uncertain origin, though possibly a derivative of Proto-Indo-European *(s)ker- (“to shrink, dry, wrinkle, bend”). Related to dialectal Norwegian skråen (“half-dried, withered, hard”), Middle Low German schra (“dry, lean”), Proto-Finnic *raha (“squirrel pelt; money”). Note in particular similarities to Proto-Germanic *skrinkwaną (“to shrink”).
From the noun skrá (Etymology 1).

From Proto-Germanic *skōhaz, of unclear etymology; possibly a derivation from *skehaną (“to move quickly”), from Proto-Indo-European *skek- (“to move quickly, jump”). Compare Old English scōh, Old Frisian skōch, skō, Old Saxon skōh, Old High German scuoh, Gothic 𐍃𐌺𐍉𐌷𐍃 (skōhs).
From skera.
From skera.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *sneumô.
From Proto-Germanic *sōl (“sun”).
From Proto-Germanic *spurjaną.

From Proto-Germanic *standaną. The verb occurred in two forms within Germanic, but only this form is attested in Old Norse sources. In the east, this form existed side by side with unattested *stá (from which Swedish stå), from Proto-Germanic *stāną. Both ultimately derive from the same Proto-Indo-European *steh₂-.

From Proto-Norse ᛊᛏᚨᛁᚾᚨᛉ (stainaʀ), from Proto-Germanic *stainaz (“stone”). Cognate with Old English stān, Old Frisian stēn, Old Saxon stēn, Old High German stein, Gothic 𐍃𐍄𐌰𐌹𐌽𐍃 (stains).
Ultimately from Pre-Germanic *stoyh₂nos, o-grade of Proto-Indo-European *steyh₂- (“to stiffen”).
glosses: a stone building, cloister, cell (especially of an anchoret)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *stigô. Related to stíga.

From Proto-Germanic *stōlaz (“stool, chair, throne”). Cognate with Old English stōl, Old Frisian stōl, Old Saxon stōl, Old Dutch stuol, Old High German stuol, Gothic 𐍃𐍄𐍉𐌻𐍃 (stōls). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *stāl- (“frame, rack, stand”). The second meaning is a calque of Latin sēdēs (“seat”), while the third is presumably a calque of Latin thronī (“the Thrones”).

From Proto-Germanic *sūpaną.
From Proto-Germanic *swarō. Related to sverja (“to swear”).
From Proto-Germanic *sundą (“swimming”)
From earlier syngva, from Proto-Germanic *singwaną.

From Proto-Norse ᛊᚹᛖᛊᛏᚨᚱ (swestar), from Proto-Germanic *swestēr, from Proto-Indo-European *swésōr (“sister”). Compare Old Saxon swestar, Old English sweoster, Old High German swester, Gothic 𐍃𐍅𐌹𐍃𐍄𐌰𐍂 (swistar).

Derived from systir (“sister”) (or more correctly an old adjectival form of it, *swes(t)riga-) with the suffix *-īn(i)a indicating a relation between people as in the words feðgin (“a father and his daughter”) and mǿðgin (“a mother and her son”). Unrelated to the English kin.
From sýnn.
Deverbative. From sýna + -ing.
From Proto-Germanic *takaną, an ablaut variant of *tēkaną (“to touch”).
From Proto-Germanic *takaną, an ablaut variant of *tēkaną (“to touch”).
From Proto-Germanic *taskǭ.

From Proto-Germanic *tilą (“goal”). Cognate with Old English til, Old Frisian til, German Ziel n (“goal”). The preposition has arisen from an adverbial use of the noun, lit. "(with) the goal of something"; this is also the reason it takes the genitive.

From Proto-Germanic *tīmô, from Proto-Indo-European *tī-, from *dī- (“time”). Cognate with Old English tīma (whence English time). The Germanic source is related to Proto-Germanic *tīdiz (“time”), whence Old Norse tíð.
From Proto-Germanic *trūwāną.

From Proto-Germanic *werþaną, from Proto-Indo-European *wert- (“to turn”). Cognate with Old English weorþan, wurþan, Old Frisian wertha, Old Saxon werthan, Old Dutch werthan, Old High German werdan, Gothic 𐍅𐌰𐌹𐍂𐌸𐌰𐌽 (wairþan).
From Proto-Germanic *þū (“you; thou”), from Proto-Indo-European *túh₂ (“you; thou”).

From earlier *hánn, from Proto-Norse *ᚺᚨᚾᚨᛉ (*hanaʀ /⁠hānaʀ⁠/), of uncertain origin. Perhaps a compound of *he- (corresponding to English he) and *ānaʀ (from Proto-Germanic *jainaz, corresponding to Gothic 𐌾𐌰𐌹𐌽𐍃 (jains)) or even directly from *ānaʀ with h- added due to influence from the demonstrative pronoun hinn, or else perhaps directly inherited from Proto-Indo-European and answering to A...

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *wiþiz, *wiþjǭ (“cord, rope”), from Proto-Indo-European *weyt- (“that which winds or bends, branch, switch”), from Proto-Indo-European *wey- (“to turn, wind, bend”).
From Proto-Germanic *wiþi, a shortened form of Proto-Germanic *wiþrą (“against”), from Proto-Indo-European *wi-tero- (“more apart”), from *wí (“separation”).
From Proto-Norse *ᚦᚨᛁᛉ (*þaiʀ), from Proto-Germanic *þai (“they, those”), plural masculine of *sa (“that”). Cognate with Gothic 𐌸𐌰𐌹 (þai).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *þō (“they, those”), plural neuter of *sa (“that”). Cognate with Old English þā, Old Saxon thē, Old High German diu, Gothic 𐌸𐍉 (þō).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse ᚦᚨᚱ (þar), Proto-Germanic *þar (“there”). Cognate with Old English þār, þǣr, Old Frisian thēr, dēr, Old Saxon thār, Old High German dār, Gothic 𐌸𐌰𐍂 (þar).

From Proto-Germanic *hwar (“where”), formed with a locative suffix from the stem of *hwaz (“who”). Cognate with Old English hwær, Old Frisian hwēr, Old Saxon hwar, Old Dutch war, wara, Old High German war, Gothic 𐍈𐌰𐍂 (ƕar).

From Proto-Germanic *twai, whence also Old English twā (English two), Old Frisian twā, Old Saxon twā, twō, Dutch twee, German zwei, Gothic 𐍄𐍅𐌰𐌹 (twai). Compare also Old English twēġen (English twain), Old Saxon twēne, Old High German zwēne (German zween). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dwóh₁, whence also Latin duo, Ancient Greek δύο (dúo).

From Proto-Norse ᚦᚱᛁᛃᛟᛉ (þrijoʀ) (feminine plural), from Proto-Germanic *þrīz (“three”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *tréyes (“three”). Cognate with Old English þrī, þrīe, Old Frisian thrē, thriā, Old Saxon thrīe, Old Dutch thri, Old High German drī, Gothic 𐌸𐍂𐌴𐌹𐍃 (þreis).

From Proto-Germanic *fedwōr, from *kʷetwṓr, the neuter form of Proto-Indo-European *kʷetwóres. The elder form with -ð- is still visible in the name of Fjaðryndaland (Old Swedish Fiæþrundaland). Proto-Germanic intervocalic d was pronounced ð.
From Proto-Germanic *fimf, whence also Old English fīf, Old High German fimf. Ultimately from *pémpe, variant of Proto-Indo-European *pénkʷe.
From Proto-Germanic *ahtōu, from Proto-Indo-European *oḱtṓw.

From Proto-Germanic *tehun (“ten”), from Proto-Indo-European *déḱm̥ (“ten”).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *agōną, related to agi (“terror”), from Proto-Germanic *agô, form of *agaz (“fear, dread”).
Possibly from Proto-Germanic *harisjaz (“army’s leader”), from Proto-Germanic *harjaz (“army”).
glosses: a local chief lord (up until about 1050)
genitive plural indefinite of foss m

entries.etymology: Nominal formation related to liggja (“to lie”).

glosses: strong masculine accusative singular of settr

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *uhsô, from Proto-Indo-European *uksḗn (“bull”).
From Old East Slavic търгъ (tŭrgŭ, “trade, trading, commerce; (trade) square”), from Proto-Slavic *tъrgъ.

glosses: strong masculine/neuter genitive singular of bendr

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *laubą, perhaps from Proto-Indo-European *lewp- (“peel, break off”). Cognate with Old English lēaf, Old Saxon lōf, Old High German loup, Gothic 𐌻𐌰𐌿𐍆𐍃 (laufs).

glosses: first-person singular present active indicative of taka

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *tanhwō (“tough ground”).

glosses: indefinite singular feminine accusative of danskr
indefinite plural masculine accusative of danskr
definite singular masculine oblique of danskr

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *hweulō (plural of *hwehwlą, whence hvél), from Proto-Indo-European *kʷékʷléh₂, plural of *kʷekʷlóm, *kʷékʷlos (“wheel”). Compare Old English hwēol, Old Saxon hwiol, Old Frisian hwēl.
Perhaps a Uralic/Finnic borrowing, from or related to the source of Finnish piika (“maid”).
glosses: a girl, a lass, a virgin
a young girl, a working girl, a virgin, a lass, a spinster

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *aurijaz, from Latin aureus (“gold coin”).
glosses: an ounce (usually of silver); equivalent to an eighth of a mark (mǫrk) or sixty pennies (penningar)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *wiliþō, from *wiljaz; see vili.
From Proto-Germanic *werką, from Proto-Indo-European *wérǵom, from Proto-Indo-European *werǵ-. Cognate with Old English weorc.

glosses: strong superlative degree masculine accusative singular of fár

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *fram, from Proto-Indo-European *pro- (“forth, forward”).

glosses: first-person singular present active indicative of láta

entries.etymology: Related to stríð.
glosses: positive degree strong feminine accusative singular
positive degree strong masculine accusative plural
positive degree weak masculine oblique singular

entries.etymology: Related to stríð.
From Proto-Germanic *strīdijaną. Cognate with English stride.

glosses: positive degree weak masculine nominative singular of lekr
third-person singular/plural present subjunctive of leka
third-person singular/plural present subjunctive of leka

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *nēhwiz, comparative of *nēhw. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂neḱ- (“to reach”).
From Proto-Germanic *nēhwiz, comparative of *nēhw. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂neḱ- (“to reach”).
glosses: in accordance with, in conformity to

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *nēhwiz, comparative of *nēhw. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂neḱ- (“to reach”).
From Proto-Germanic *nēhwāną, related to (or derived from) Proto-Germanic *nēhwaz (“near”). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂neḱ- (“to reach”).
glosses: to get hold of, reach, overtake
to be able to, to be allowed to

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse ᛁᚾᚨᚾᚨ (inana), from Proto-Germanic *innanē, akin to inn + -an.
glosses: from within, from an internal part

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse ᛁᚾᚨᚾᚨ (inana), from Proto-Germanic *innanē, akin to inn + -an.
From Proto-Germanic *uzdaz (“pointing upward”), whence also Old English ord, Old High German ort. Cognate of dialectal English ord (“a point”).
glosses: sharp point, especially of a weapon

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *fiskaz (“fish”). Cognate with Old English fisc, Old Frisian fisk, Old Saxon fisk, Old Dutch fisk, Old High German fisk, Gothic 𐍆𐌹𐍃𐌺𐍃 (fisks). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *peysk-.

Etymology tree
Proto-Indo-European *dʰegʷʰ-?
Proto-Germanic *dagaz
Old Norse dagr
Inherited from Proto-Germanic *dagaz, possibly from Proto-Indo-European *dʰegʷʰ-.

From Proto-Norse *ᚱᚨᚢᛞᚨᛉ (*raudaʀ), from Proto-Germanic *raudaz, whence also Old English rēad, Old Frisian rād, Old Saxon rōd, Old High German rōt, Gothic 𐍂𐌰𐌿𐌸𐍃 (rauþs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁rowdʰós < *h₁rewdʰ-.
From Proto-Germanic *hwītaz, whence also Old English hwīt, Old Saxon hwīt, and Old High German wīz. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ḱweytos.
From Proto-Germanic *swartaz, whence also Old English sweart, Old High German swarz, Gothic 𐍃𐍅𐌰𐍂𐍄𐍃 (swarts). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *swordo- (“dirty, dark, black”).
From Proto-Germanic *rakōjaną.
From Proto-Germanic *rīsaną, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁rey-.

glosses: first-person singular present indicative active of sá

entries.etymology: Borrowed from Middle Low German schūr, from Proto-Germanic *skūrō, whence also Old English scūr (English shower), Middle Dutch schuur, Old High German scūr, Gothic 𐍃𐌺𐌿𐍂𐌰 (skūra).
From Proto-Germanic *seuþaną (“to seethe”).

From Proto-Germanic *skarpaz, whence also Old English sċearp, Old Frisian skerp, skarp, Old Saxon skarp, Old Dutch skarp, Old High German scarf. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kerb-, from *(s)ker- (“to cut”).
From Proto-Germanic *seglą.

From Proto-Germanic *selbaz, whence also Old English self, seolf, Old Frisian self, Old Saxon self, Old High German selb, Gothic 𐍃𐌹𐌻𐌱𐌰 (silba). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *selbʰ- (“one's own”), from *s(w)e- (“separate, apart”).
From Proto-Germanic *samaz (“same, alike”), whence also Old High German sama. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *somHós, made from the root *sem- (“together, one”).
From Proto-Germanic *samaz (“same, alike”), whence also Old High German sama. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *somHós, made from the root *sem- (“together, one”).
From Proto-Germanic *seukaz. Cognate with Old English sēoc (whence English sick), Old High German sioh (whence German siech).

From Proto-Germanic *braidaz, whence also Old English brād (English broad), Old Frisian brēd (West Frisian breed), Old Saxon brēd (Low German breed, breet), Dutch breed, Old High German breit (German breit), Gothic 𐌱𐍂𐌰𐌹𐌸𐍃 (braiþs).
From Proto-Germanic *daudaz (“dead”), whence also Gothic 𐌳𐌰𐌿𐌸𐍃 (dauþs), Old English dēad and Old High German tōt.
From Proto-Germanic *dauþuz (“death”).
From Proto-Germanic *deupaz.

From Proto-Germanic *fagraz, whence also Old English fæġer ( > English fair), Old Saxon fagar, Old High German fagar, Gothic 𐍆𐌰𐌲𐍂𐍃 (fagrs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ph₂ḱ- (“to fasten, place”). Non-Germanic cognates include Latin pulcher and Slovak pekný (“nice”).
From Proto-Germanic *fastuz; see it for cognates and further etymology.
glosses: fast, firm, sticking fast to the spot

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *gōdaz, whence also Old Saxon, Old Frisian, and Old English gōd, Old High German and Old Dutch guot, Gothic 𐌲𐍉𐌸𐍃 (gōþs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʰedʰ-. The comparative and superlative forms are from Proto-Germanic *batizô and *batistaz respectively, both degrees of Proto-Germanic *bataz, being ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰed- (“good”).

From Proto-Germanic *harduz, whence also Old English heard, Old Frisian herd, Old Saxon hard, Old Dutch hart, Old High German hart, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐍂𐌳𐌿𐍃 (hardus). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *kert-, *kret- (“strong; powerful”).
From Proto-Germanic *haitaz, whence also Old English hāt, Old Saxon hēt, Old High German heiz.
From Proto-Germanic *kaldaz, whence also Old English cald, Old Frisian kald, Old Saxon kald, Old High German kalt, Gothic 𐌺𐌰𐌻𐌳𐍃 (kalds). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gel-.
From Proto-Germanic *langaz, whence also Old English lang, Old High German lang.
glosses: long, far, distant (of distance and length)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *lataz, whence also Old English læt (“slow, sluggish, late”), Old High German laz (“slow, lazy”).
From Proto-Germanic *lēgaz, whence also Dutch laag. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *legʰ-.
From Proto-Germanic *linhtaz, whence also Old English lēoht, Old High German līhti, liht. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁lengʷʰ-.
From Proto-Germanic *rehtaz, whence also Old English riht, Old High German reht. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₃reǵtós.
From Proto-Germanic *rehtuz, related to Etymology 1.
From Proto-Germanic *sanþaz Proto-Indo-European *h₁s-ónt-s (“being, existing”), present participle of *h₁es- (“to be, exist”). The change -nnr > -ðr is regular. Compare with maðr.
From Proto-Germanic *setiją, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sed-.
From Proto-Germanic *smiþaz, whence also Old English smiþ, Old High German smid.
From Proto-Germanic *sanþaz, whence also Old English sōþ.

glosses: first/third-person singular past active indicative of spinna

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *mannz, whence also Old Saxon mann, Old English mann, Old High German man, Gothic 𐌼𐌰𐌽𐌽𐌰 (manna). Probably ultimately from a Proto-Indo-European root *mon-. The change *-nnz > -ðr in the nominative singular is a regular feature of Old Norse; compare with aðrir (m.nom.pl. of annarr), guðr and saðr.
From Proto-Germanic *sakōną.
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
From Proto-Germanic *spinnaną.
From Proto-Germanic *starāną (“to gaze, observe”). Cognate with Old English starian (modern English stare).

From Proto-Norse ᛋᛏᛡᛒᛡ (stᴀbᴀ) (accusative plural), from Proto-Germanic *stabaz, whence also Old English stæf, Old High German stap. Some forms from an i-stem variant *stabiz, whence also Gothic 𐍃𐍄𐌰𐍆𐍃 (stafs).
glosses: letter (of the Latin or Runic alphabet)

entries.etymology: Inherited from Proto-Germanic *stadiz (“place, location”). Cognate with Old English stede (whence English stead), Old Frisian stede, Old Saxon stedi, Old Dutch stede, stat (whence Dutch stad), Old High German stat, Gothic 𐍃𐍄𐌰𐌸𐍃 (staþs).
From Proto-Germanic *stadaz. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *steh₂- (“to stand”).
From Proto-Germanic *starkuz, whence also Old English stearc, Old High German stark.
From Proto-Germanic *bōguz, whence also Old English bōg, Old High German buog. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰeh₂ǵʰús.
From Proto-Germanic *būkaz, whence also Old High German būh (German Bauch).

From Proto-Germanic *draumaz, whence also Old English drēam (perhaps related to Modern English dream), Old Frisian drām (West Frisian dream), Old Saxon drōm (Low German Droom), Old High German troum (Modern German Traum). i-umlaut in descendant languages is from levelling with the verb dreyma.
From Proto-Germanic *drunkiz, whence also Old English drync and Old High German trunch.
glosses: a drink, the act of drinking

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse *ᚨᛁᚦᚨᛉ (*aiþaʀ) (attested in the name ᚨᛁᚦᚨᛚᚨᛏᚨᛉ (aiþalataʀ)), from Proto-Germanic *aiþaz, whence also Old English āþ (English oath), Old Frisian ēth, Old Saxon ēth, Old High German eid, Gothic 𐌰𐌹𐌸𐍃 (aiþs).
From Proto-Germanic *sturmaz, whence also Old English storm, Old Saxon storm, Old High German sturm. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *(s)twer-, *(s)tur- (“to rotate, swirl, twirl, move around”).
From Proto-Germanic *sturkaz, whence also Old English storc, Old High German storh.
From Proto-Germanic *strangaz, whence also Old Saxon and Old English strang, Old High German strengi, strangi. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *strengʰ- (“taut, stiff, tight”).

From Proto-Germanic *straumaz, from Proto-Indo-European *srowmos, from *srew- (“to flow”). Compare Old English strēam (English stream), Old Frisian strām (West Frisian stream), Old Saxon strōm (Low German Stroom), Dutch stroom, Old High German stroum, strōm (German Strom (“a stream, a current”)).
glosses: a stream, a current, a race, a river
accusative/dative singular indefinite of strengr m

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *strangiz, whence also Old English streng, Old High German stranc. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *strengʰ- (“rope, cord; to be tight”).
From Proto-Germanic *friþuz. Cognate with Old English friþ, Old High German fridu.
glosses: an agreement not to use violence or launch lawsuits in religious shrines, and at certain times like holidays and during plantation and harvest

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *sūganą, whence also Old English sūgan, Old Saxon sūgan, Old High German sūgan. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sug-, *suk-.
From Proto-Germanic *sumaz, whence also Old English sum, Old High German sum, Gothic 𐍃𐌿𐌼𐍃 (sums). See also English some.
From Proto-Germanic *sundraz, whence also Old English sunder, Old High German suntar.
From Proto-Germanic *hōfaz, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱoph₂ós. Germanic cognates: see Proto-Germanic *hōfaz. Non-Germanic cognates include Russian копыто (kopyto) and Sanskrit शप्ह (śapha).
From Proto-Germanic *sunþrą, whence also Old English sūþ, Old High German sunt.
From Proto-Germanic *sunþrą, whence also Old English sūþ, Old High German sunt.
From Proto-Germanic *austrą, whence also Old English ēast, Old High German ōst, ōstar.
From Proto-Germanic *westrą, whence also Old English west, Old High German west.
From Proto-Germanic *nurþrą, whence also Old English norþ, Old High German nord.
From Proto-Germanic *nurþrą, whence also Old English norþ, Old High German nord.
From Proto-Germanic *swelganą, whence also Old English swelgan, Old High German swelhan.
From Proto-Germanic *swarmaz, whence also Old English swearm, Old High German swarm. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *swer- (“to buzz, hum”).
From Proto-Germanic *sweltaną.

From Proto-Germanic *gardaz (“court, yard, enclosure”).
Cognate with Old English ġeard, Old Saxon gard, Old High German gart, Gothic 𐌲𐌰𐍂𐌳𐍃 (gards).

From Proto-Germanic *helmaz, whence also Old English helm (whence English helm), Old Saxon helm (whence Low German Helm), Old Frisian helm (whence West Frisian helm), Old High German helm (whence German Helm), Gothic 𐌷𐌹𐌻𐌼𐍃 (hilms).
From Proto-Germanic *swa, whence also Old English swā, Old High German sō.
From Proto-Germanic *taumaz, whence also Old English tēam, Old High German zoum.
From Proto-Germanic *tamaz.
From Proto-Germanic *taglą, whence also English tail.
From Proto-Germanic *hrugjaz. Cognate with Old English hryċġ (English ridge), Old High German rucki.
Compare with hryggva, hryggja.

From Proto-Germanic *hundaz, whence also Old Saxon, Old English, Old Frisian, and Old Dutch hund, Old High German hunt, Gothic 𐌷𐌿𐌽𐌳𐍃 (hunds). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ḱʷn̥tós, from *ḱwṓ (“dog”).
From Proto-Germanic *timrą. Akin to English timber.
From Proto-Germanic *triwwiz, whence also Old English trīewe, Old High German triuwi.
From Proto-Germanic *þauh (“nevertheless, though”).
From Proto-Germanic *þauh (“nevertheless, though”).

glosses: first-person/third-person singular past indicative active of þvá

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *þurpą, whence also Old High German dorf (German Dorf), Old English þorp, þrop, Gothic 𐌸𐌰𐌿𐍂𐍀 (þaurp).
From Proto-Germanic *þakjaną, whence also Old English þeċċean, Old Saxon thekkian, Old High German decchen.
From Proto-Germanic *þekuz, whence also Old English þicce, Old Saxon thikki, Old Frisian thikke, Old High German dicki. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *tégus (“thick”).
From Proto-Germanic *þrēduz, from Proto-Indo-European *treh₁-tu-, from *terh₁- (“rub, twist”). Germanic cognates include Old English þræd, Old Frisian thrēd, Old Saxon thrād, Old High German drāt.
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
From Proto-Germanic *þreskaną. Compare Old English þrescan (English thresh), Dutch dorsen, Old High German dreskan (German dreschen), Gothic 𐌸𐍂𐌹𐍃𐌺𐌰𐌽 (þriskan).
Borrowed from Middle Low German hōp, from Old Saxon hōp, from Proto-West Germanic *haup, from Proto-Germanic *haupaz.
From Proto-Germanic *þunnuz, whence also Old English þynne. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ténh₂us.
From Proto-Germanic *upp (“up”). Cognate with Old English upp, Old Frisian up, Old Saxon up, Old High German ūf, Gothic 𐌹𐌿𐍀 (iup).
From Proto-Germanic *waikwaz, whence also Old English wāc, Old High German weih.
From Proto-Germanic *nabalô (“navel”). Cognate with Old English nafola, Old Frisian navla, Old Saxon *navalo, Old Dutch *navalo, Old High German nabalo.

From Proto-Germanic *wegaz, whence also Old English weġ (English way), Old Saxon weg (Low German Weg), Old Dutch weg (Dutch weg), Old High German weg (modern German Weg), Gothic 𐍅𐌹𐌲𐍃 (wigs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *weǵʰ-.

glosses: to blaze; to burn with a flame

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *walþuz (“forest”). Cognate with Old English weald, wald, Old Frisian wald, Old Saxon wald, Old High German wald. According to Kloekhorst, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *wólnus (“meadow, pasture”) and cognate with Hittite 𒌑𒂊𒂖𒇻𒍑 (wellu-š, “pasture, meadow”).
From Proto-Germanic *wedrą. Cognates include Old English weder and Old High German wetar (from Proto-West Germanic *wedr).
From Proto-Germanic *wilþijaz, whence also Old English wilde, Old Frisian wild, Old Saxon wildi, Old High German wildi, Gothic 𐍅𐌹𐌻𐌸𐌴𐌹𐍃 (wilþeis).
From Proto-Germanic *wiljô, whence also Old English willa, Old High German willa.
From Proto-Germanic *wiljô, whence also Old English willa, Old High German willa.
glosses: third-person singular/plural present active subjunctive of vilja

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *wanatōną.
glosses: to cause to lack [with accusative ‘something’] (idiomatically translated as "lack, be lacking" with the accusative object as the subject)

entries.etymology: From earlier vrangr, from Proto-Germanic *wrangaz, akin to the Common Germanic verb *wringan.
From earlier vreiðr, from Proto-Germanic *wraiþaz, whence also Old English wrāþ (> English wroth), Old High German reid.

From Proto-Germanic *warmaz, whence also Old English wearm, Old Saxon warm, Old Frisian warm, Old High German warm. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʷʰer- (“warm, hot”) or alternatively *wer- (“to burn”).
From Proto-Germanic *lagjaz (“leg, thigh”), from Proto-Indo-European *(ǝ)lak-, *lēk- (“leg, the main muscle of the arm or leg”). Cognate with Lombardic lagi (“thigh”).

glosses: second/third-person singular present indicative active of leggja

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *wabjaz or *wabją, whence also Old English webb, Old High German weppi.
From Proto-Germanic *waibijaną, whence also Gothic 𐌱𐌹𐍅𐌰𐌹𐌱𐌾𐌰𐌽 (biwaibjan).
From Proto-Germanic *webaną, from Proto-Indo-European *webʰ- (“to weave”).
From Proto-Germanic *wartǭ, whence also English wart.
From Proto-Germanic *wunskijaną, whence also Old English wȳscan, Old High German wunsken.
From Proto-Germanic *berhtaz, whence also Old English beorht, Old Saxon berht, Old High German beraht, Gothic 𐌱𐌰𐌹𐍂𐌷𐍄𐍃 (bairhts).
From Proto-Germanic *anþeraz (“second, other”). Cognate with Old English ōþer, Old Frisian ōther, Old Saxon ōthar, Old Dutch andar, ander, Old High German andar, Gothic 𐌰𐌽𐌸𐌰𐍂 (anþar).
From Proto-Germanic *armaz, whence also Old English arm, Old High German arm.
From Proto-Germanic *armaz.
From Proto-Germanic *akraz, whence also Old English æcer, Old High German ackar, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂éǵros.
From Proto-Germanic *aldrą (whence also Old English ealdor, Old High German altar), from Proto-Indo-European *h₂el- (“to grow”).
From Proto-Germanic *allaz (“all”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₂el- (“all”). Cognate with Old English eall, Old Frisian all, Old Saxon al, Old Dutch al, ol, Old High German al, Gothic 𐌰𐌻𐌻𐍃 (alls).
From Proto-Germanic *alaz, akin to Old English æl and Old High German ala. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁ólos.
From Proto-Norse ᚨᚱᛒᛁᛃᚨᚾᛟ (arbijano /⁠arbijanō⁠/) (genitive plural), from Proto-Germanic *arbijô.
From *arbijaz, whence also Old English ierfe, Old High German arbeo.
From Proto-Germanic *argaz, whence also Old English earg, Old High German arg.
From Proto-Germanic *jungaz, whence also Old Saxon jung, Old English ġeong, Old High German junc, Gothic 𐌾𐌿𐌲𐌲𐍃 (juggs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂yuh₁n̥ḱós.

From Proto-Germanic *rīkijaz, a derivative of *rīks (“king, ruler”), itself a borrowing from Proto-Celtic *rīxs, from Proto-Indo-European *h₃rḗǵs. Compare Old English rīċe, Old Saxon rīki, Old High German rīhhi, Gothic 𐍂𐌴𐌹𐌺𐍃 (reiks). See also the related term ríki.

From Proto-Germanic *fullaz, whence also Old English full (English full), Old Frisian full (West Frisian fol), Old Saxon ful (Low German vull), Dutch vol, Old High German foll (German voll), Gothic 𐍆𐌿𐌻𐌻𐍃 (fulls). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *pl̥h₁nós.
From Proto-Germanic *tōmaz.
From Proto-Germanic *tōmijaną, related to the adjective tómr (“empty”).
Past participle of þreyta.
From Proto-Germanic *þrangwaz.
From Proto-Germanic *þunguz, from Proto-Indo-European *téngʰus, akin to Proto-Slavic *tęžьkъ (compare Serbo-Croatian težak) and Lithuanian tingus. All from Proto-Indo-European *tengʰ-.
From Proto-Germanic *stuntaz, whence also Middle High German stunz.

From Proto-Germanic *swalaz (“cool, [originally: burningly] cold”), from *swelaną (“to burn slowly, create a burningly cold sensation”), from Proto-Indo-European *swel- (“to shine, warm up, burn”). Akin to German schwül (“sultry”), Dutch zwoel (“sultry”), and Ancient Greek σέλας (sélas, “luminous”).
From Proto-Germanic *blautaz, whence also Old English blēat and Old High German blōz.
From Proto-Germanic *wētaz, whence also Old English wǣt, Old Frisian wēt.
From Proto-Germanic *þurstiz, *þurstuz. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ters- (“dry”).
From Proto-Germanic *hunhruz, whence also Old English hungor, Old Saxon hungar and Old High German hungar.
From Proto-Germanic *dapraz, whence also Old High German tapfar.
From Proto-Germanic *dankwaz, cognate with Old High German tunkal. No relation to English dark.
From Proto-Germanic *wandaz (“twisted, turned”), an o-grade adjective from *windaną (“to wind”).
From Proto-Germanic *friskaz, whence also Old English fersc, Old Frisian fersk, Old High German frisc. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *preysk-.
From Proto-Germanic *auþijaz (“void, empty”), whence also Old English ēaþ and Old High German ōdi, whence German öde, Öde and Einöde.
From Proto-Germanic *audaz (“goods, possession, luck”). Cognate with Old English ēad, Old Saxon ōd, Old High German ōt, the first part of Gothic 𐌰𐌿𐌳𐌰𐌷𐌰𐍆𐍄𐍃 (audahafts, “fortunate”).

From Proto-Germanic *askaz, whence also Old English æsc, Old High German ask.
For the semantic shift from "tree" to "container" compare Latin buxus, buxis (whence English box, boxwood).

Etymology tree
Proto-Celtic *letrosbor.
Proto-Germanic *leþrą
Old Norse leðr
From Proto-Germanic *leþrą, whence also Old English leþer and Old High German ledar.
From Old English bāt (“boat”). Displaced native Old Norse beit (“boat”).
From Proto-Germanic *bankiz, whence also Old English benc (English bench), Old High German bank (German Bank).
From Proto-Germanic *bitraz, whence also Old English biter (English bitter), Old Saxon bittar (Low German bitter), Old High German bittar (German bitter).
From Proto-Germanic *blēstuz.
From Proto-Germanic *kussaz, whence also Old English coss, Old Saxon kus, kos, Old High German kus.

From Proto-Germanic *aftrą, *aftrē, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂epoteros, whence also Old English æfter, Old High German aftar. Compare also eptir. Proto-Germanic *aftrē is probably related to Proto-Germanic *afteraz, *aftraz, and *afarē.
From Proto-Germanic *ankurô, from Latin ancora.
From Proto-Germanic *bebruz, whence also Old English befer, Old High German bibar.

From Proto-Germanic *baunō, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰabʰ- (“bean”). Compare Old English bean (English bean), Old Frisian bāne (West Frisian bean), Old Saxon bōna (Low German Bohn), Dutch boon, Old High German bōna (German Bohne).
From Proto-Germanic *þurskaz. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ters- (“dry”).
From Proto-Germanic *aikwernô, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *wer-. Compare Old English ācweorna, Old Saxon ēkhorn, Old High German eihhorno.
From Proto-Germanic *arþrą n, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂érh₃trom, whence also Latin aratrum.
From Proto-Germanic *falþaz, from Proto-Indo-European *pel- (“to fold”), see also Old English fealdan, Old High German falton.
glosses: border, edging, hem (usually on the lower end of a garment)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *habrô, whence also Old Saxon haƀoro, Old High German habaro.
From Proto-Norse ᚺᛡᛒᛟᚱᚢᛗᛦ (hᴀborumʀ /⁠haβᵒrumʀ⁠/) (dative plural), from Proto-Germanic *hafraz, from Proto-Indo-European *kapro-. Cognate with Old English hæfer, Latin caper.
From Proto-Germanic *naglaz, whence also Old English næġl, Old High German nagal.
Ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *tabulā, from Latin tabula (“a tablet; a board or plank”). Cognate with Old English tæfl (“game board; board game”).
From Proto-Germanic *brūwō.
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

From Proto-Norse *ᚷᚨᛊᛏᛁᛉ (*gastiʀ) (attested in several name compounds), from Proto-Germanic *gastiz, whence come also Old English ġiest, Old High German gast. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʰóstis.
From Proto-Germanic *liþuz, whence also Old English liþ, Old High German lid.
From Proto-Germanic *nēþō-, from Proto-Indo-European *net- (“to favor, protect”), cognate with Old Saxon ginātha, Old High German gināda.
From Proto-Germanic *habukaz, whence also Old English hafoc, Old Saxon havuk, Old High German habuch. Probably ultimately derived from Proto-Indo-European *kap- (“seize”).
From Proto-Germanic *hataz, whence also Old English hete, Old Saxon heti, Old High German haz, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐍄𐌹𐍃 (hatis).
From Proto-Germanic *habjaną, from Proto-Indo-European *kh₂pyéti, from the root *kap- (“to seize”).
Inherited from Proto-Germanic *hūdiz, from Proto-Indo-European *kuHtis.
From Proto-Germanic *huljaną.
From Proto-Germanic *humaraz (“lobster”).
From Proto-Germanic *daubaz, whence also Old English dēaf, Old High German toub.
From Proto-Germanic *kraftuz.
From Proto-Germanic *limuz, whence also Old English lim.
From Proto-Germanic *matiz. Cognate with Old English mete (whence meat), Old High German maz.

From Proto-Germanic *midjaz (“middle, mid”), from Proto-Indo-European *medʰyo- (“middle”). Cognate with Old English midd, Old Frisian midde, Old Saxon middi, Old High German mitti, Gothic 𐌼𐌹𐌳𐌾𐌹𐍃 (midjis).

From earlier minnr, by a regular sound change -nnr- > -ðr-, also observed e.g. in maðr, suðr, syðri, Guðrún (< *Gunnrún). Cognate with Gothic 𐌼𐌹𐌽𐍃 (mins), Latin minus, from Proto-Indo-European *mey- (“small, little”).

From Proto-Germanic *sunnǭ (“sun”).
Compare Norn sjiner, Shetlandic Scots sjiner (literally “shiner”).
glosses: based on descendants:(Runic alphabet) name of the rune ᛋ (s)

entries.etymology: Inherited from Proto-Germanic *nīþą. Cognate of Gothic 𐌽𐌴𐌹𐌸 (neiþ), German Neid, Dutch nijd.
glosses: contumely (offensive and abusive language or behaviour; scorn, insult), derision (act of treating with disdain), scornful mockery, belittling, disparaging, reproach
libel (false statement which unjustly seeks to damage someone's reputation, the act or tort of displaying such a statement publicly)
insult by carving a person's likeness (tréníð, “wood níð”) on an upraised post or pole (níðstǫng, “níð pole”)
derision (something to be derided, treating with disdain; a laughing stock; compare with níðingr)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *neutaną (“to use, make use of; to enjoy”), from Proto-Indo-European *newd- (“to seize; grasp; use”).
glosses: to enjoy; to have the use or benefit of
to derive benefit from, profit by
to cause to be available, to cause to be at hand, to cause to be present [with genitive ‘something’] (idiomatically translated as "be at hand, be present" with the genitive object as the subject)
to be due to, to result from
to cause to be of help [with genitive ‘something’ and at (+ clause) ‘that ...’] (idiomatically translated as "help, be of help" with the genitive object as the subject)

entries.etymology:
A short form of Old Norse given names with Ás-, Æs-.
Ultimately from Old Norse áss (“god”), from Proto-Germanic *ansuz (“god”).
From Proto-Germanic *ansaz ("beam").
glosses: a thick pole, main beam (in a house)

entries.etymology: Probably related to Old Frisian dōk, Old Saxon dōk or Middle Low German dōk; ultimately from Proto-Germanic *dōkaz.
From Proto-Germanic *justaz. Akin to Latin ius, Proto-Slavic *juxa. Compare Finnish juusto.
From Proto-Germanic *silubrą, whence also Old English seolfor, Old Saxon siluvar, Old High German silbar, Gothic 𐍃𐌹𐌻𐌿𐌱𐍂 (silubr).
From Proto-Germanic *kwikwaz, whence also Old English cwic, Old High German quec. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʷih₃wós (“alive”).
From Proto-Germanic *dwergaz (“dwarf”). Cognate with Old English dweorg, Old Frisian dwerch, Old Saxon dwerg, Old High German twerg, twerc.
From Proto-Germanic *gaukaz, akin to Old English ġēac, Old High German gouh.
Of Germanic origin, related to Proto-Germanic *staupijaną (“to pour, soak”).
From Proto-Germanic *haigrô.

From Proto-Germanic *rugiz, whence also Old English ryġe. Old Frisian rogga, Old Saxon roggo, Old Dutch *roggo, and Old High German rocko are from the related West Germanic form *roggō. Cognate with Russian рожь (rožʹ) and Old Prussian rugis.
From Proto-Germanic *rukkô, akin to Old High German rocko.
From Proto-Germanic *drupô. Compare Old English dropa, Old High German troffo, tropfo. Related to (and probably ultimately derived from) the verb drjúpa (“to drip; to bow (one’s head)”).
From Proto-Germanic *brūdiz, whence also Gothic 𐌱𐍂𐌿𐌸𐍃 (brūþs), Old Saxon brūd, Old English brȳd (English bride), Old High German brūt (German Braut).
From Proto-Germanic *baltiją, *baltijaz, from Latin balteus.
From earlier of sinni, from Proto-Germanic *gasinþô (“companion”). Cognate with Old English ġesīþ, Old Saxon gisīth, Old High German gisind, gisindo, Gothic 𐌲𐌰𐍃𐌹𐌽𐌸𐌰 (gasinþa), 𐌲𐌰𐍃𐌹𐌽𐌸𐌾𐌰 (gasinþja).
From Proto-Germanic *wīdaz, whence also Old English wīd, Old Frisian wīd, Old Saxon wīd, Old High German wīt.
From Proto-Germanic *gulaz, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰelh₃-.
Possibly from an accusative case form of Proto-Germanic *langį̄.
From Proto-Germanic *hugiz, whence also Gothic 𐌷𐌿𐌲𐍃 (hugs), Old English hyġe, Old High German hugu, Dutch heug.

glosses: strong masculine/neuter genitive singular of saltr (“salty”)

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *nautą, related to Old English nēat, Old High German nōz (whence dialectal German Noss).

glosses: first-person singular past indicative active of njóta
third-person singular past indicative active of njóta

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse ᚺᚨᚱᚨᛒᚨᚾᚨᛉ (harabnaʀ/hᵃraƀᵃnaʀ), from Proto-Germanic *hrabnaz, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ḱorh₂-. Cognate with Old English hræfn (English raven), Old Frisian ravan (West Frisian raven), Old Saxon hravan, ravan (Low German Raav), Old Dutch ravo (Dutch raaf), Old High German raban (German Rabe). Compare also Latin corvus and Ancient Greek κόραξ (kórax).

From Late Latin clocca, probably of Celtic origin, from Proto-Celtic *klokkos (“bell”) (compare Welsh cloch, Irish clog), from Proto-Indo-European *klek- (onomatopoeia).
Akin to Old High German klocca.
From Proto-Germanic *haitaną, from Proto-Indo-European *key(w)-, *kyew- (“to set in motion”). Cognate with Old English hātan, Old Saxon hētan, Old High German heizzan, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌹𐍄𐌰𐌽 (haitan).
glosses: to be called, to be named

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *sunuz (“son”), from Proto-Indo-European *suHnús (“son”). Cognate with Old English sunu, Old Frisian sunu, Old Saxon sunu, Old High German sunu, Gothic 𐍃𐌿𐌽𐌿𐍃 (sunus).
From Proto-Germanic *samdaz (“sand”). Cognate with Old English sand, Old Frisian sand, Old Saxon sand, Old Dutch sant, Old High German sant.
From Proto-Germanic *snaiwaz, from Proto-Indo-European *snóygʷʰos; cognates include the Old English snāw and the Old High German snēo.
From Proto-Germanic *snigilaz.
From Proto-Germanic *sukkaz (whence also Old English socc, Old High German soc), from Latin soccus.

From Proto-Germanic *skulaną, from Proto-Indo-European *skel- (“to be obligated, owe, be guilty”). Cognate to Old English sculan (“to be obligated or obliged to, shall, must, owe, ought to”) (English shall).
From Proto-Germanic *diurijaz, whence also Old English dīere (English dear), Old High German tiuri.
From Proto-Germanic *herutaz, whence also Old English heorot, Old High German hiruz.
From Proto-Germanic *hirdijaz. Agent noun to hirða.

From Proto-Germanic *hwaitijaz, from *hwītaz (“white”).
Compare Old English hwǣte (English wheat), Old Saxon hwēti (German Low German Weite, Weit), Dutch weit, Old High German hwaizi, weizi (German Weizen), Gothic 𐍈𐌰𐌹𐍄𐌴𐌹𐍃 (ƕaiteis).

From Proto-Norse ᚹᛁᚾᛁᛉ (winiʀ), from Proto-Germanic *winiz (“friend; loved one”), from Proto-Indo-European *wenh₁is, from the root *wenh₁- (“to seek, desire, love, win”). Compare Old English wine, Old Frisian wine, Old Saxon wini, Old High German wini.
From Proto-Norse *ᚹᛁᛞᚢᛉ (*widuʀ), from Proto-Germanic *widuz (“wood”). Cognate with Old English widu, wudu, Old Saxon widu, Old High German witu.
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
glosses: second/third-person singular present active indicative of vinna

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *niþer, whence Old English niþer, Old High German nidar.
From Proto-Germanic *niþjaz, cognate with Gothic 𐌽𐌹𐌸𐌾𐌹𐍃 (niþjis).
glosses: ancestors; deceased men belonging to the same clan

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *grēwaz.
From Proto-Germanic *bazaz, whence also Old English bær, Old High German bar. Cognate with Icelandic ber and Norwegian Bokmål bar. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰosós.
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
glosses: second/third-person singular present indicative of bera
second/third-person singular present indicative of berja

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *waljaną. Ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *welh₁-.
From Proto-Germanic *saiwiz (“sea, ocean”). Cognate with Old English sǣ, Old Frisian sē, Old Saxon sēo, Old High German sēo, Gothic 𐍃𐌰𐌹𐍅𐍃 (saiws).
See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *blindaz, whence also Old English blind, Old High German blint.
Likely borrowed from Old Northern French ker, kier, quer (Old French chier, cher), from Latin cārus (“dear”), from Proto-Italic *kāros, from Proto-Indo-European *keh₂- (“to desire, to wish”).

From earlier glíkr, from Proto-Germanic *galīkaz, whence also Gothic 𐌲𐌰𐌻𐌴𐌹𐌺𐍃 (galeiks), Old English ġelīc, Old High German gilīh. Compare gnógr, nógr (“enough”) for another word which in early texts retained g- from Proto-Germanic *ga-.
From einn (“one”) + -gi (“not”), literally “not one”. Compare Old Norse ekki.
From einn (“one”) + -gi (“not”), literally “not one”. Compare Old Norse ekki.
From Proto-Germanic *nakwadaz, from Proto-Indo-European *negʷ- (“naked, bare”); see there for the cognates.
From Proto-Germanic *sūraz, whence also Old English sūr, Old High German sūr. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *súHros.

From Proto-Germanic *maizô, whence also Old English māra (English more), Old Frisian māra (West Frisian mear), Old Saxon mēro (Low German mehr), Dutch meer, Old High German mēro (German mehr), Gothic 𐌼𐌰𐌹𐌶𐌰 (maiza).

From Proto-Germanic *maizô, whence also Old English māra (English more), Old Frisian māra (West Frisian mear), Old Saxon mēro (Low German mehr), Dutch meer, Old High German mēro (German mehr), Gothic 𐌼𐌰𐌹𐌶𐌰 (maiza).
From Proto-Germanic *maistaz, superlative form of *mikilaz (“great, large, many”).
From Proto-Germanic *magraz, whence also Old English mæġer, Old High German magar (“thin; meager”). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *mh₂ḱrós.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *slihtaz, whence also Old High German sleht.
From Proto-Germanic *sebun (whence also Old English seofon, Old High German sibun, Gothic 𐍃𐌹𐌱𐌿𐌽 (sibun)), from earlier *sebunt, from Proto-Indo-European *septḿ̥.
From Proto-Germanic *fōts, whence also Old Saxon fōt, Old English fōt, Old High German fuoȥ, Gothic 𐍆𐍉𐍄𐌿𐍃 (fōtus). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *pṓds.
From Proto-Germanic *ebnaz.

From Proto-Germanic *brewwaną.
From Proto-Germanic *bringô, which is related to *brinkaz (“edge, hill”).
From Proto-Germanic *þulāną.
From Proto-Germanic *aiganaz, past participle of *aiganą, whence also eiga.
From Proto-Germanic *aiz n, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂éyos n.
From Proto-Germanic *fleuhaną.
From Proto-Germanic *fēgōną, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *peḱ- (“joyful, pretty”).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *faigijaz, whence also Old English fæġe (English fey)), Old High German feigi (“cowardly”).
From Proto-Germanic *þistilaz.

From Proto-Germanic *frekaz, whence also Old English frec, Old High German freh, Middle High German vrech, German frech.
Also the root for the wolf Freki of Norse Mythology.

From Proto-Germanic *geldaną, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰeldʰ- (“to pay”).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse *ᛗᛁᚾᚨᛉ (*minaʀ /⁠mīnaz⁠/) (several non-lemma forms attested), from Proto-Germanic *mīnaz, whence also Old English mīn, Old High German mīn.
From Proto-Germanic *þīnaz, whence also Old English þīn (English thine, thy), Old High German dīn (German dein), Gothic 𐌸𐌴𐌹𐌽𐍃 (þeins).

From Proto-Germanic *ganōgaz, whence also Old English ġenōg, Old Saxon ginōg, Old High German ginuog, Gothic 𐌲𐌰𐌽𐍉𐌷𐍃 (ganōhs). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂eh₂nóḱe (“he has reached, attained”), perfective of *h₂neḱ- (“to reach”).
From Proto-Germanic *hradaz, whence also Old English hræd, Old High German hrad.
From Proto-Germanic *gladaz, whence also Old English glæd (English glad), Old High German glat.
From Proto-Germanic *grīpaną.
From Proto-Germanic *hailagaz (“holy, sacred”), whence also Old English hāliġ, Old Frisian hēlich, Old Saxon hēlag, Old High German heilag, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌹𐌻𐌰𐌲𐍃 (hailags). Akin to heill + -agr.

From Proto-Germanic *lausaz, whence also Old English lēas, Old High German lōs; from Proto-Indo-European *lewH-, *lū- (“to untie, set free, separate”), whence also Ancient Greek λύω (lúō).
From Proto-Germanic *miltiją, from Proto-Indo-European *(s)meld- (“to beat, grind, crush, weaken”).
From Proto-Germanic *mildijaz, whence also Old English milde, Old Saxon mildi, Old High German milti, Gothic 𐌿𐌽𐌼𐌹𐌻𐌳𐌴𐌹𐍃 (unmildeis).
From Proto-Germanic *helpaną, whence also Old English helpan, Old Frisian helpa, Old Saxon helpan, Old High German helfan, Gothic 𐌷𐌹𐌻𐍀𐌰𐌽 (hilpan). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ḱelb-, *ḱelp-.
From Proto-Germanic *hulaz, whence also Old English hol, Old High German hol.
From Proto-Norse ᚺᚨᚺᚨ (haha), from Proto-Germanic *hah(w)ō, cognate with Faroese hógvur and Norwegian Nynorsk hå.

entries.etymology: Proto-Germanic *mūgô, from Proto-Indo-European *muk- (“heap”), similar to Ancient Greek μύκων (múkōn, “pile”). Compare Old English mūga.

From Proto-Germanic *slahaną, from Proto-Indo-European *slak- (“to hit, strike, throw”).
Compare Old English slēan (English slay), Old Frisian slā, Old Saxon slahan (Low German slaan), Dutch slaan, Old High German slahan (Low German schlagen), Gothic 𐍃𐌻𐌰𐌷𐌰𐌽 (slahan).
From Proto-Germanic *smalaz, whence also Old English smæl, Old High German smal.
From Proto-Germanic *gēz (“yesterday”).
From Old English cawel.

From Proto-Norse *ᛗᚨᚾᛟ (*mano), from Proto-Germanic *mēnô (“the moon”), from Proto-Indo-European *mḗh₁n̥s (“moon, month”). Cognate with Old English mōna, Old Frisian mōna, Old Saxon māno, Old High German māno, Gothic 𐌼𐌴𐌽𐌰 (mēna).
Inherited from Proto-Germanic *sa, from Proto-Indo-European *só. Related to Old Norse sjá.
From Proto-Germanic *sēaną, from Proto-Indo-European *seh₁-. Compare Old English sāwan (English sow), Old Saxon sāian, Old High German sāen, sāwen (German säen), Gothic 𐍃𐌰𐌹𐌰𐌽 (saian).
From Proto-Indo-European *bʰren- (“project”), related to Old English brant (“steep”), Latvian bruôds (“roof ridge”).
Of uncertain origin, but possibly related to English blight, which see.
From Proto-Germanic *bukkaz (“buck”).
From Proto-Germanic *dala-, whence also Old English dæl, Old High German tal.
Borrowed from a West Germanic source, perhaps Old English disc, from Proto-West Germanic *disk.
From Proto-Germanic *dōmaz, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰóh₁mos. Compare Old English dōm, Old Frisian dōm, Old Saxon dōm, Old High German tuom, Gothic 𐌳𐍉𐌼𐍃 (dōms).

From Proto-Norse *ᛞᚨᚱᚨᚢᚷᚨᛉ (*dᵃraugaʀ, “revenant, ghost, phantom; deceiver”), from Proto-Germanic *draugaz (“delusion, mirage, illusion”). Akin to Old Saxon gidrog (“delusion”) and Old High German bitrog (“delusion”), gitrog (“ghost”). See also Finnish raukka.
Also borrowed into the Sami-languages through an earlier Proto-Norse form, see Proto-Samic *rāvkë.

Later, use of this suffix was extended to create adjectives from verbs, such as Old Norse lyginn (“prone to lying”), from ljúga (“to lie”...

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *Harjawaldaz, from *harjaz (“army”) and *waldaz (“leader”). Compare *Waldaharjaz (“Walther, Walter”), composed of the same elements but in the reverse order. Attested by Tacitus, in the form Chariovalda. Cognates include English Harold, French Héraut, Italian Araldo, Spanish Haraldo.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse ᛊᛏᚨᛁᚾᚨᚹᚨᚱᛁᛃᚨᛉ (stainawarijaʀ), consisting of ᛊᛏᚨᛁᚾᚨᛉ (stainaʀ, “stone”) and Proto-Norse *ᚹᚨᚱᛃᚨᛉ (*warjaʀ, “defender”). In some cases, the second part of Steinarr might stem from *ᚺᚨᚱᛃᚨᛉ (*harjaʀ, “warrior”), being equivalent to Old High German Steinheri.
From guð (“god”) and brandr (“fire, sword”).

Cognate to Ger- as in German Gerhard and English Gerard and to Old English gār as in English Edgar.

*Aiwa- is related to Proto-Germanic *aiwaz (“long time”), cognate to Latin aevum (“life, lifetime”).
*Aina- stems from Proto-Germanic *ainaz (“one”) and is cognate to Latin ūnus (“one”).

The second part (-arr) stems from Proto-Germanic *harjaz (“warrior”), whence also Old Norse herr (“army, crowd”).
Partly merged with Old Norse Yngvarr, Ingvarr.

From Proto-Germanic *Sigiwarduz, equivalent to sigr (victory) + varðr, vǫrðr (guard). Cognates include Old High German Sigiwart (German Siegwart), Old English Sigeweard (English Siward), West Frisian Sieuwerd, Dutch Sjoerd, Sjoert.

It may also be the root of Dellingr (“the father of Dagr or Day”), whose name could mean "the shining one." According to Pokorny, the root i...

From Proto-Germanic *fōdijaną (“to feed, give birth”). Cognate with Old English fēdan, Old Frisian fēda, Old Saxon fōdian, Old High German fuoten, Gothic 𐍆𐍉𐌳𐌾𐌰𐌽 (fōdjan).

entries.etymology: Ultimately from the verb (see above), perhaps via an intermediate Proto-Germanic *fōdijǭ (“food, nourishment”). Compare Middle English fede (“food, feed”), from Middle English feden (“to feed”).

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *metą (“measure”). Cognate with Old English met, Old Saxon met, Old High German mez.
From Proto-Germanic *aldiz (“age, lifetime”). Cognate with Gothic 𐌰𐌻𐌳𐍃 (alds).
From Proto-Germanic *unþiz, *unþī (“wave”). Cognate with Old English ȳþ, Old Saxon ūthia, Old High German undea, unda.
glosses: a wave (an undulation in a body of water)

entries.etymology: Clipping of ævagi, from a form related to Proto-Germanic *aiwaz (“ever”), whence also æ (“ever”), + -gi (“negative suffix”). Literally 'not ever'.

Compound of ginnunga + gap (“gap, empty space”). The first element is of uncertain origin and meaning and has been explained in a number of ways. (1) It is related to Proto-Germanic *ginnaną, from which *biginnaną (“to begin”) descended, thus meaning "primeval". (2) The prefix ginn- (for instance in ginnheilagr (“most holy”), used of the supreme gods), therefore giving the meaning "mighty, vast". ...

Traditionally derived from Proto-Germanic *ēdrô (“quickly, promptly”), from *ēdraz (“quick, prompt”) and thus cognate with Old English ǣdre, Old Frisian ēdre, Old Saxon ādro, Old High German ātar, but this doesn't fit well semantically. A better derivation is from Proto-Norse *āriʀ, from Proto-Germanic *airiz (“earlier”) and thus cognate with Gothic 𐌰𐌹𐍂𐌹𐍃 (airis, “earlier”) and English ere.
Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰer- (“to bear, carry”), therefore probably meaning "the one brought forth". Compare Buri (“the bringer forth”).
Probably from Proto-Germanic *bedō.

From miðr (“middle”) + garðr (“yard; enclosured land; realm”) or directly from Proto-Germanic *midjagardaz (“Middle Earth”). Cognate with Old English middangeard, Old Saxon middilgard, Old High German mittilgart, Gothic 𐌼𐌹𐌳𐌾𐌿𐌽𐌲𐌰𐍂𐌳𐍃 (midjungards).
glosses: the Earth, (especially Norse mythology) Midgard, Middle Earth, the world that humans (mostly) live in

entries.etymology:
From Proto-Germanic *saliz (“house, hall”). Cognate with Old English sele and Gothic *𐍃𐌰𐌻𐍃 (*sals), first part of Old Frisian selskip, also Old Saxon seli, Old High German sali and first part of selihūs and selihof.
Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *sol-, *sel- (“human settlement, village, dwelling”).
glosses: longhouse of the Viking Period, typically a one room house, 15-75 meters long, 5-7 meters wide, with two rows of columns along the length inside, supporting a wood or turf roof, and bulging wider and taller around the middle. https://www.lifeinnorway.net/viking-longhouses/

entries.etymology: Related to hlaða (“to load, pile, stack”)
From Proto-Germanic *kweþaną (“to say”), from Proto-Indo-European *gʷet- (“to say, speak”).
From Proto-Germanic *hōgiz (“skilled”), in ablaut-relationship with Proto-Germanic *hagaz (“capable, suitable”), ancestor of Old Norse hagr.
From Proto-Germanic *edaraz (“enclosure”). Cognate with Old English eodor, Old Saxon eder, Old High German etar.
glosses: the highest one, foremost one, lord

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *rakō (“unravelling, tale”). Cognate with Old English racu, Old Saxon raka, Old High German rahha, racha.
From rǫk (“fate, judgment”) + stóll (“throne, seat”).
From ginn- (“very, mighty”) + heilagr (“holy, sacred”).

From Proto-Germanic *undurniz. Cognate with Old English undern, Old Frisian undern, Old Saxon undorn, Old High German untarn, untorn, the first part of Gothic 𐌿𐌽𐌳𐌰𐌿𐍂𐌽𐌹𐌼𐌰𐍄𐍃 (undaurnimats, “breakfast, midday meal”).
Of obscure origin. Possibly related to niðr (“down”); one hypothesis derives it from Proto-Germanic *nidwō.
glosses: based on compounds and descendants: waning, decrement and thereof

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *ēbanþs (“evening”). Cognate with Old English ǣfen, Old Frisian ēvend, Old Saxon āvand, Old Dutch avont, Old High German aband.

From Proto-Germanic *harugaz (“sanctuary, cairn, grove”). Cognate with Old English hearg, hearga, Old High German harug, haruc, haruch. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ḱarǝk- (“fenced or enclosed area”).
From Proto-Germanic *tangō (“tong”). Cognate with Old English tang, tange, Old Frisian tange, tonge, Old Saxon tanga, Old High German zanga.

Compound of *iða + vǫllr (“field”). Concerning the former term, when compared to Old Norse *iðja, Swedish id, ide (“yew”), then related to Old Norse ýr, it can refer to green.
Some sources propose the meaning "splendour", while others connect it to Old Saxon idis or to the name of mount Ida.

From Proto-Germanic *taitaz (“serene, tender”). Cognate with Old English tāt, Old Saxon tēt, Old High German zeiz.
Related to teiti.

From Proto-Germanic *wanaz (“absent”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁weh₂- (“to leave, abandon, give out”). Cognate with Old English wana, Old Frisian wan, won, Old Saxon wan, Old High German wan, Gothic 𐍅𐌰𐌽𐍃 (wans).
Singular of vanir. Related to Proto-Germanic *wanaz (“accustomed”), from Proto-Indo-European *wenh₁- (“to love”).
From Proto-Germanic *mekō (“big, great”), from Proto-Indo-European *meǵh₂- (“great”).
Of obscure origin, but possibly related to ámr (“black, loathsome”), used of a giant.
From jǫtunn (“jotun, giant”) + heimr (“home, earth, world”).
From vættr (“being, wight”) + -gi.

From Proto-Norse *ᛞᚱᛟᚺᛏᛁᚾᚨᛉ (*drohtinaʀ), from Proto-Germanic *druhtinaz (“leader, lord”). Cognate with Old English dryhten, Old Frisian drochten, Old Saxon drohtin, druhtin, Old High German trohtin, truhtin. See also Finnish ruhtinas, South Karelian ruohtina.
Probably related to brim (“surf”).
Probably related to blár (“blue, dark”).
Of obscure origin. Some sources propose the meaning “battle-roarer”, while others suggest derivation from móðr (“tired, weary”).

entries.etymology: Either an independent word from Proto-Germanic *margaz related to mor and merja, or derived from mangr (from Proto-Germanic *managaz (“many, much”)) under the influence of such words. Cognate with Old English maniġ, Old Frisian monich, Old Saxon manag, Old Dutch manag, Old High German manag, Gothic 𐌼𐌰𐌽𐌰𐌲𐍃 (manags).

Either an independent word from Proto-Germanic *margaz related to mor and merja, or derived from mangr (from Proto-Germanic *managaz (“many, much”)) under the influence of such words. Related to norðr (“north”), thus meaning “One in the North”.
Related to suðr (“south”), thus meaning “One in the South”.

Related to vestr (“west”), thus meaning “One in the West”.

Probably related to nið (“waning moon”), thus meaning “Waning Moon”.

entries.etymology: Of obscure origin and meaning, although meanings “Handsome One” or “Nimble One” are proposed.
Perhaps from Old Frisian *bever (“beaver”), ultimately from Proto-Germanic *bebruz (“beaver”), thus meaning "The Beaver", "The Diligent One".

Possibly from bumba (“drum, barrel”), thus meaning "The Swollen One".

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *medumō (“middle”).

Possibly from annarr (“other, another”), thus meaning “The Other One”.
Possibly from óask (“to fear, dread”), thus meaning “The Shy One”.
From mjǫðr (“mead”) + vitnir (“unknown”), thus meaning “Mead-vitnir”, “Toper”.
Probably from veggr (“wall, wedge”), thus meaning “The Wedge”.
From vindr (“wind”) + alfr (“elf”), thus meaning “Wind-elf”.
From þora (“to dare, have the courage”), thus meaning “Bold One”.
From þrár (“stubborn, obstinate”), thus meaning “Stubborn One”.
Probably from þrár (“stubborn, obstinate”), thus meaning “Stubborn One”.
From þekkr (“agreeable, beloved”), thus meaning "Beloved One".
From litr (“colour”), especially "red colour of the face", thus meaning “Red-faced One”.
From vitr (“wise”), thus meaning “Wise One”.
From nýr (“new”), thus meaning “New One”.
Related to nýráðliga (“oddly, queerly”), thus meaning “Ingenious One”.
Likely related to regin (“the gods, the powers”), thus meaning “Potent One”.
From ráð (“advice, counsel”) + sviðr (“swift, wise”), thus meaning “Wise in Counsel”.
Possibly from Old Saxon or Old High German fīla (“file”), thus meaning “The Filer”.
Possibly from kíll (“wedge”), related to Old High German kīla (“wedge”), thus meaning “The Wedge”.
From fundinn (“found”), past participle of finna (“to find”), thus meaning “Found One”.
Possibly from nál (“needle”), thus possibly meaning “The Needle”.
Uncertain and meaning, but possibly a misspelling for Fíli, meaning “The Needle”.
From hepti (“handle”), thus possibly meaning “The Handle”.
Possibly from hannarr (“skilled”), thus meaning “Skilled One”.
Possibly related to svína (“to dwindle, subside”), thus meaning “Dwindled One”.
From billingr (“twin brother”).
Probably related to brúnn (“brown”), thus meaning “Brown One”.

Probably from bíldr (“edged weapon, bloodletting knife”), thus meaning "The Edged Weapon". Ultimately from Proto-Germanic *bīþla- (“axe”), instrumental noun from *bītaną (“to bite”).
However, compare *bilją (“blade, axe”), which could have influenced its development.
From Proto-Germanic *burô (“bearer; one who carries or brings”), Proto-Indo-European *bʰer- (“to bear, carry”). Compare Borr (literally “the one brought forth”).
From frár (“swift”), thus meaning “Swift One”.
From horn (“horn”) + bora (“to bore, drill”), thus meaning “Horn-borer”.
From frægr (“famous”), thus meaning “Famous One”.

From aurr (“clay, mud”) + vangr (“wong, field”), thus meaning "Mud-wong".
From eikinn (“oaken”) + skjǫldr (“shield”), thus meaning “One with Oaken Shield”.
Unknown. Probably of imitative origin.

From Proto-Germanic *wangaz (“field, meadow, slope”). Cognate with Old English wang, wong, Old Saxon wang, Old High German wang, Gothic 𐍅𐌰𐌲𐌲𐍃 (waggs).
From Proto-Germanic *auraz (“wet sand or earth, mud”). Cognate with Old English ēar and possibly Gothic 𐌰𐌿𐍂𐌰𐌷𐌾𐍉𐌼 (aurahjōm), an inflection of an obscure Gothic word.
Probably from jara (“fight, battle”) + vǫllr (“vale, field”), thus meaning "Battle-vale".
From Proto-Germanic *setō. Cognate with Old English set.
Probably related to drjúpa (“to drip”), therefore meaning "Dripper" (of rings, thus a metaphor for goldsmith).
From dolgr (“enemy, fiend”) + þrasir (“snorter, one in rage”), the second element deriving from þrasa (“to snort, rage”), thus meaning “Battle-eager”.
From hár (“high”), thus meaning "High One".
From haugr (“burial mound, cairn”) + spor (“track, footprint”), thus meaning “Howe-treader”.
From hlé (“shelter, lee”) + vangr (“wong, plain, meadow”), thus meaning “Sheltered Plain”.
From glóa (“to glow”), thus meaning “Glowing One”.

Possibly related to skirpa (“to spit”), thus meaning “The Spitter”.
Possibly related to Old Frisian verver (“dyer”), thus meaning “The Dyer”, or created as a rhyme for Skirfir.
From skár (“open, good”) + viðr (“tree wood, board”), thus possibly meaning “Good Tree”.
From ái (“great-grandfather”), thus meaning “Great-grandfather”.
From ginna (“to dupe, deceive”), thus meaning "The Deceiver".
Probably related to frost (“frost”), thus meaning “Cold One”.
Possibly related to fjǫl (“plank, deal, board”), therefore meaning "The Paneller". Another possibility is the derivation from fela (“to hide, conceal”), thus meaning "The Spy".

entries.etymology:
From Proto-Germanic *fuþiz (“vagina”).
Found in words such as fuþflogi ("cunt-fleer", a man who avoided women and marriage) and in attested nicknames such as fuþkula (cunt-ball), fuþsleikir (cunt-licker) and silkifuþ (silky-cunt). It is also found by itself, for example in the runic inscription NB011: "felleg er fuþ sin bylli [fuþorglbasm]" (Wonderful is the cunt, may the cock fill it up!).
See al...

The first element is cognate to Latin Carpates and displays the effects of Grimm's law, making this the sole attestation in any Germanic language of the inherited Germanic name of this mountain range. Tentatively, the Proto-Germanic reflex of the Carpathian ethnonym may thus have been *harbaþaz. The second element, fjǫll, is simply the plural of fjall (“mountain”).

entries.etymology:
From Proto-Norse ᛊᚾᚨᚷᚹᚢ (*snaggwu-), from Proto-Germanic *snawwaz (“rapid, sudden”).
Compare Dutch snugger (“smart”), which is likely related and may be borrowed from a North Germanic language. However, this is disputed by Etymologiebank, as the Dutch cannot be related to the Norse via direct inheritance from Proto-Germanic, and that a mutual borrowing is unlikely.

From Proto-Norse *ᚨᛁᚾᚨᛚᛁᛒᚨᛉ (*ainalibaʀ) or Proto-Norse *ᚨᛁᚹᚨᛚᛁᛒᚨᛉ (*aiwalibaʀ).

entries.etymology: Derived from faðma + -na + -ir, literally “fathomer”. Others relate it to Old Norse gamall or Icelandic góa. Compare Old Norse gymbr and Icelandic gimbill, gimbur and Swedish gumse. Possibly ultimately derived from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰéyōm 'winter'.

Inherited from Proto-Norse ᚺᚨᚷᚢᛊᛏᚨᛚᛞᚨᛉ (hagustaldaʀ), inherited from Proto-Germanic *hagustaldaz. Cognate with Old English hagosteald. The initial hauk- is irregular and probably due to folk etymology.
From Proto-Norse *ᚨᚾᚢᛚᚨᛁᛒᚢ (*anulaibu), from Proto-Germanic *Anulaibō.

Assumed to stem from a root with the sense "running, leeping", originally meaning "scurrying creature" or thereof, then cognate with Middle High German tremen (“wobble”), to Proto-Germanic *tremaną (“to wander, waver”); also Old English trem (“track”), Ancient Greek δραμε͂ιν (dramêin, “run”), Sanskrit द्रमति (drámati, “to run (about)”).
Compare þrá.
From Proto-Germanic *Dōmaharjaz, from *dōmaz (“judgement”) + *harjaz (“army”), equivalent to dóm + herr, cognate with Old English Dōmhere.
From Proto-Germanic *Harjawardaz from *harjaz (“army”) + *wardaz (“guard”). Equivalent to herr + varðr. Cognate with Old English Hereweard.
From Þórr (“Thor”) + bjǫrg (“Salvation”).

Uncertain, speculations include: a derivation from beyla (“hump”), from baula (“cow, barn woman”) and from baun (“bean”) through a Proto-Germanic *baunilō which would make her a fertility goddess, cf. Byggvir from bygg (“barley”) from Proto-Germanic *bewwu. Cognate with Old English Bēow.
glosses: A goddess, wife of Byggvir, servant of Freyr

entries.etymology: From naddr (“spear, weapon”) + oddr (“point”).
glosses: The first Norseman to set foot in Iceland, according to the Landnámabók by Ari Þorgilsson.

entries.etymology: From Proto-Germanic *Herzendô or Proto-Germanic *Herrendô from Proto-Germanic *herzô (“hinge”) therefore possibly meaning "the hinged one" or rather "the hanged one", equivalent to hjara + -andi, cf. Old Norse hjarr (“hinge”) or maybe "the loud one", cf. Shetland sjara (“to rattle”). Cognate with Old English Heorrenda which is soley attested in Deor where Heorrenda is a genius poet, replacing Deor...
From Proto-Germanic *Wulfaharjaz, of Proto-Germanic *wulfaz (“wolf”) and *harjaz (“army, warrior”). Cognate with Old English Wulfhere. Also compare Galician Gulfar.

Uncertain, cognate with Old English Fitela and possibly Old High German Sintarvizzilo. Ásgeirr Blöndal Magnússon suggests a derivation from Proto-Germanic *Sindrafetilô meaning "Sparkling feet", equivalent to sindr + fet + -ill
Possibly cognate with Old English Bēowulf, probably derived from Bēow (“Beow”) + wulf (“wolf”) which may be interpreted as "the wolf of Beow".

Uncertain, Finn Magnusson equates it with þjálfi (“worker”), some have compared it to Þjelfar from Guta saga, Schröder derives it from Proto-Germanic *Þelfô from Proto-Indo-European *telbʰ- from Proto-Indo-European *(s)tel- (“to drip”), Olrik equated it with Þjelfar and gave the etymology as þel + fari, Guðbrandur Vigfússon suggested that it's related to English delve, some equate it with þjálmi (...
glosses: Þjálfi, Þórr’s servent companion, brother of Rǫskva

entries.etymology:
Unknown. Related to Middle English scalle, English scall, scald (“scurf, rash”).
The form skolð assumed on Old Swedish skoldh.
From bǫð (“battle”) + móðr (“wrath”).
From Middle Low German Dēdrīk, from Proto-Germanic *Þeudōrīks, equivalent to the inherited Þjóðrekr.
glosses: Dietrich von Bern, a character in Þiðreks saga af Bern

entries.etymology:
From a contracted form of faðir (“father”) as the secondary element in proper names or from an archaic declension of the same word, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *fadēr. The nominative u-umlaut ǫ would thus be from analogy with the oblique cases.
An alternative explanation advanced by Kuhn and other scholars is that the root is Proto-Germanic *fadiz (“master”), changed to an u-stem. In this case ...
from bygg (“barley”) from Proto-Germanic *bewwu cognate with Old English Bēow.
glosses: A god, husband of Beyla, servant of Freyr

entries.etymology: From Proto-Norse *Ingwôhildiz, equivalent to Yngvi (“God Yngvi”) + hildr (“battle”)
